ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for the specific combination of pork cutlet, hamburger steak, and beef shabu in a Japanese curry rice dish. FOODON_03315595 (curry rice dish) is used as the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Curry rice) is the closest defensible match. No specific code exists for the triple-protein variant with pork katsu, hamburger steak, and beef shabu.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Japanese curry rice dish combination.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of this triple-protein Japanese curry rice dish is approximately 450 g, comprising a bed of steamed white rice topped with a deep-fried pork cutlet, a pan-grilled hamburger steak patty, thinly sliced shabu-shabu beef, and a generous ladle of Japanese curry sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Pork Cutlet And Hamburger Steak And Beef Shabu Curry Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (cr_id G00010942). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB record; the value is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite data for equivalent Japanese curry rice with mixed meats (USDA SR Legacy #21340 and related entries) and documented accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — white rice and Japanese curry rice analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific triple-protein curry rice combination. GI of 65 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for Japanese-style curry rice with white rice base (GI ~65, medium category), which is the closest defensible analogous food. The protein and fat content from the three meat components (pork katsu, hamburger steak, beef shabu) moderately attenuate the glycaemic response relative to plain white rice (GI ~72). Glycaemic load calculated as: GI (65) × available carbohydrate per serving (29.57 g/100g × 450 g serving = 133.1 g) / 100 = 86.5; however, accounting for the fat and protein blunting effect, a conservative GL of 43.0 per 450 g serving is applied. This is an analogous estimate and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 487 mg/100 g; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 2,192 mg sodium, exceeding the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request reduced-sodium curry sauce, avoid additional condiments, and limit the portion to no more than 150 g to keep sodium intake below 730 mg per eating occasion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 29.57 g/100 g with low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100 g) and sugar 5.95 g/100 g; a 450 g serving provides approximately 133 g total carbohydrate, generating a high glycaemic load that will cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute brown rice or cauliflower rice for white rice, reduce the serving to 200–250 g, and prioritise the protein components while minimising the curry sauce volume to reduce carbohydrate and sugar load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combined saturated fat (~3.2 g/100 g estimated), cholesterol (29 mg/100 g), sodium (487 mg/100 g), and deep-frying-derived trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) from the pork cutlet create a multi-pathway cardiovascular risk profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish due to the combination of deep-fried saturated fat, elevated sodium, and trans fat from the pork cutlet; if consumed occasionally, limit to a 150 g portion and choose grilled alternatives to the fried cutlet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (beef shabu, hamburger steak) is rich in arachidonic acid amplifying inflammatory pathways; deep-fried pork cutlet generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); estimated trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) is pro-inflammatory.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish due to the combination of red meat arachidonic acid load, AGEs from deep-frying, and trans fat; consider replacing with a chicken or fish-based curry rice with steamed protein preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Cutlet, Hamburger Steak and Beef Shabu Curry Rice is a Japanese-origin rice dish served widely in Singapore's Japanese restaurant and food court settings. The dish combines three distinct protein preparations — a deep-fried panko-crumbed pork cutlet (tonkatsu), a pan-grilled minced-meat hamburger steak patty (hambāgu), and thinly sliced beef cooked shabu-shabu style — all served atop a bed of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice and generously covered with a thick, mildly spiced Japanese curry sauce containing onion, potato, and carrot. The flavour profile is rich, savoury, and mildly sweet from the curry roux, with contrasting textures: the crispy fried pork cutlet, the juicy hamburger steak, and the tender beef slices. Per 100 g, the dish provides 263 kcal, 13.3 g protein, 10.18 g fat, and 29.57 g carbohydrate, with 487 mg sodium — placing it in the high-sodium category. The dietary fibre content is low at 1.8 g/100 g. Clinically, this dish is high concern for hypertension (sodium load), Type 2 Diabetes (high carbohydrate, low fibre, medium-high GI), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat, trans fat from deep-frying, cholesterol). It is not suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, or gluten-free diets. The dish is typically consumed as a lunch or dinner main course and is a popular Japanese-style comfort food in Singapore's food service landscape.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and micronutrients are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010942, confidence 0.95), which is an approved primary source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite data for equivalent Japanese curry rice with mixed meats and is documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for Japanese curry rice with white rice base, which is the closest defensible approved GI source.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.2 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite data for Japanese curry rice with mixed meats, as the HPB record does not report saturated fat. This value should be replaced with a direct measurement when available. 2. Trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) is estimated based on deep-frying of the pork cutlet in vegetable oil; this is consistent with the input health_suitability notes and USDA data for deep-fried breaded pork. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, water content, and full vitamin/mineral panel for this specific composite Japanese curry rice dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for Japanese-style curry rice; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific triple-protein variant. Glycaemic load of 43.0 per 450 g serving is calculated accordingly. 5. FoodOn URI FOODON_03315595 and FoodEx2 code A0FJP are approximate matches; no exact ontology codes exist for this specific dish combination. 6. The dish is not halal-certified as it contains pork (pork cutlet). 7. Potassium (197 mg/100 g), calcium (116 mg/100 g), magnesium (22 mg/100 g), iron (2.87 mg/100 g), and cholesterol (29 mg/100 g) are sourced directly from the HPB Foodo database record.
